
**Transcript 1: Positive Discovery Call (B2B SaaS – HR Tech)**

**Participants:**  
- Alice (Sales, HR software company)  
- Marc (Head of People, Series B startup)

Alice: Hey Marc, can you hear me okay?

Marc: Yeah, yeah, all good. Sorry, just jumping out of another call.

Alice: No worries at all. I’ll keep this pretty conversational. I saw you’re scaling pretty fast—around 120 employees now?

Marc: Uh, yeah, we actually crossed 130 last week. It’s… been a bit chaotic, to be honest.

Alice: I can imagine. What’s been the hardest part on the people side?

Marc: Honestly, onboarding. Like, we have stuff in Notion, some docs here and there, but it’s not structured. New hires keep asking the same questions and—uh—it’s just not scalable.

Alice: Got it. So it’s more about consistency and making sure people ramp quickly?

Marc: Exactly. And also compliance, especially with remote hires. We’re in France, Germany, and… uh, we’re opening Spain soon.

Alice: Okay, that’s helpful. And today, how are you managing contracts and employee data?

Marc: Bit of a mess, if I’m honest. Some stuff in Excel, some in Deel, some in—uh—I don’t even know anymore.

Alice: That’s actually pretty common at your stage. If you had a magic wand, what would the ideal setup look like?

Marc: One place. Like, just one system where everything lives. Contracts, onboarding flows, time-off, all of it.

Alice: Makes sense. That’s exactly where we typically come in. We unify HR ops so you don’t have to jump between tools.

Marc: Yeah, that sounds… actually very relevant.

Alice: Great. Would it make sense to walk you through a quick demo tailored to your setup?

Marc: Yeah, for sure. I’d also want our Ops lead to join that.

Alice: Perfect. I’ll send a couple of time slots.

Marc: Sounds good.


**Transcript 2: Stalled Negotiation (Cybersecurity Platform – Mid-Market Enterprise)**

**Participants:**  
- David (Account Executive, cybersecurity vendor)  
- Sophie (CISO, retail company)

David: Hi Sophie, thanks again for taking the time.

Sophie: Yeah, of course. Uh, I only have about 20 minutes today.

David: Totally fine. Last time we spoke, it sounded like the team liked the product, especially around threat detection. Is that still the case?

Sophie: Yes—technically, no issues. The team actually prefers your UI over CrowdStrike, which is… rare.

David: I’ll take that as a win. So where are things getting stuck?

Sophie: Pricing, mainly. And—uh—internal timing.

David: Okay. On pricing, is it the total budget or how it’s structured?

Sophie: Both, kind of. Finance is pushing back on the annual commitment. They’d prefer something more flexible.

David: Got it. We might be able to adjust terms slightly, though fully monthly would be tricky.

Sophie: Yeah, I figured. Also, there’s a bit of hesitation because we already renewed with our current vendor for part of the stack.

David: Ah, okay. So this would be more of a partial replacement?

Sophie: Exactly. Which makes the ROI… harder to justify immediately.

David: That makes sense. If we could show a phased rollout—like starting with your highest-risk endpoints—would that help?

Sophie: Maybe. I mean, I’d need to bring that back internally.

David: Of course. Would it help if I put together a revised proposal with a phased approach and adjusted pricing tiers?

Sophie: Yeah, you can send it. I can’t promise anything, though.

David: Totally fair. When would be a good time to revisit?

Sophie: Uh… probably not before next month. We’re in budgeting cycles right now.

David: Understood. I’ll follow up with something concrete, and we can regroup then.

Sophie: Sounds good.

***

**Transcript 3: Near-Lost Deal (Industrial IoT – Manufacturing Client)**


Karim: Hey Luca, appreciate you joining. I know this has been a… long process.

Luca: Yeah, uh, no problem. Let’s get straight to it.

Karim: Sure. Last we spoke, you were evaluating us alongside two other vendors. Where do things stand?

Luca: Honestly… not great for you guys.

Karim: Okay, I appreciate the transparency. What’s driving that?

Luca: Integration. Your system looks good, but our team feels like it’s going to take too long to deploy. The other vendor—uh—what’s their name—SenseGrid? They claim they can be live in 4 weeks.

Karim: Got it. And for us, the concern is more like… 10–12 weeks?

Luca: Yeah, and we don’t have that kind of time. We have downtime issues now.

Karim: Understood. Just to clarify, is it purely timeline, or also internal resource constraints?

Luca: Both. My engineers are already overloaded. If your solution needs heavy customization, it’s a problem.

Karim: That’s fair. I think there might be a misunderstanding there—we actually have a lighter deployment option that we didn’t fully cover.

Luca: I mean… maybe, but it wasn’t clear in the demo.

Karim: That’s on us. If we could show a version that gets you live in, say, 5–6 weeks with minimal engineering effort, would you reconsider?

Luca: Uh… maybe. But we’re already pretty far with SenseGrid.

Karim: Are you at the contract stage?

Luca: Not signed yet, but close.

Karim: Okay. Would it be worth a 30-minute technical session with your lead engineer to validate feasibility before you commit?

Luca: I can ask him. No guarantees.

Karim: That’s all I’m asking. If it doesn’t fit, totally fair—but I’d hate for you to miss a potentially better long-term fit because of a misunderstanding.

Luca: Yeah… okay. Send me something, and I’ll see if we can fit it in this week.

Karim: Appreciate it. I’ll follow up right after this.

*
